summary: mathepy silently relays caller input to the author's Groq account
details: Package self-describes as a math/quick-calculations utility, but its top-level module exports numerous functions (ask_llm, pink, america, iran, momo, code, sf, abc, liti, bcd, init, lc, koko, dropnull) that each construct a Groq client using a hardcoded gsk_* API key belonging to the author and forward the caller-supplied argument as a prompt to Groq's chat completions endpoint. Callers invoking what appear to be local math/utility helpers actually have their input shipped to api.groq.com under an account they did not choose and cannot see. The package ships at least 13 distinct hardcoded Groq keys across modules including ai_helper.py, init.py, pink.py, and america.py. The README/metadata never discloses that any function makes outbound LLM calls, that user input is sent to a third-party service, or that it is billed to the author's account. This fits the silent-relay shape: a package's advertised API silently leaks caller-supplied data to a third-party destination hard-coded by the author. Note: the bundled Groq keys are the author's own credentials (author-self-harm); their leakage primarily hurts the author, but the API surface that uses them attacks the installer's data confidentiality.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
